title: Reading Error Output
intro: When your terminal screams red text at you, most beginners panic and close the window. But error messages are actually helpful -- they tell you exactly what went wrong and where. The developer in this code read the error message but applied the wrong fix. Find the mistake.

teach.title: Error Output = A Doctor's Diagnosis
teach.body: When you go to the doctor feeling sick, they don't just say "you're sick." They give you a **diagnosis**: the symptoms (headache, fever), the cause (bacterial infection), and the location (sinus cavity). **Terminal error messages work the same way** -- they tell you the symptom (what failed), the cause (why it failed), and the location (which file and line number). Reading an error message is like reading a medical report: skip the jargon, focus on the diagnosis.

teach.title: stderr = A Car's Dashboard Warning Light
teach.body: Your car has two ways to talk to you. The speedometer shows normal info (speed, fuel) -- that's **stdout**, the standard output. But when something goes wrong, a **warning light** turns on with a specific icon telling you what's broken -- engine, battery, oil pressure. That's **stderr**, the standard error stream. Developers who ignore stderr are like drivers who put tape over their warning lights. The car still runs... until the engine explodes.

teach.title: stdout vs stderr
teach.body:
Your terminal has two separate output channels:

- **stdout (standard output)** -- Normal program output. Success messages, data, results.
- **stderr (standard error)** -- Error messages, warnings, diagnostic info.

Both appear in the same terminal window, but they're separate streams. This matters because you can redirect them independently:

`command > output.log 2> errors.log`

This saves normal output to one file and errors to another.

teach.title: Anatomy of an Error Message
teach.code:
$ npm run build

Error: Cannot find module 'react'
  Require stack:
  - /home/user/my-app/src/App.jsx
  at Module._resolveFilename (node:internal/modules/cjs/loader:1075:15)
  at /home/user/my-app/src/App.jsx:1:1
teach.annotation: Line 1: WHAT failed (can't find module 'react'). Line 2-3: WHERE it happened (App.jsx, at the require/import). Lines 4-5: The stack trace -- the chain of function calls that led to the error. Always read from top to bottom.

teach.body: Developers who can read error messages fix bugs in minutes instead of hours. That's the difference between billing a client for 30 minutes or 4 hours. In vibecoding, your AI assistant generates code, but when something breaks, YOU need to read the error and guide the fix. This skill directly translates to money saved and earned.

Error messages have three parts: what failed, why it failed, and where it failed. stdout shows normal output, stderr shows errors. Always read the FIRST error message -- later errors are often caused by the first one. The error tells you exactly what to fix.

exercise.prompt: The error clearly says 'Cannot find module react' -- the react package is missing. But the developer installed react-dom instead of react. Which line shows the wrong fix?
exercise.explanation: The error message says exactly what's missing: the 'react' module. The developer installed 'react-dom' instead -- a related but different package. react-dom handles rendering React to the browser DOM, but 'react' is the core library. The fix is simply `npm install react`. Option B (react-router) is a routing library, not the core. Option C (npm update) updates existing packages but won't install missing ones. Option D (deleting node_modules) is overkill -- the package was never listed in package.json, so reinstalling won't help.
